mission.title: Install and Configure Argo Workflows (Argo) on Kubernetes
mission.description: Argo Workflows is a powerful workflow engine for Kubernetes that allows you to orchestrate parallel jobs and manage complex workflows using container-native technology. Installing Argo Workflows enables you to define and run workflows for various use cases such as machine learning, data processing, and CI/CD.

steps.title: Add the Argo Workflows Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Argo Workflows Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add argo https://argoproj.github.io/argo-helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Argo Workflows using Helm
steps.description:
Install Argo Workflows in the `argo` namespace, creating the namespace if it doesn't exist:
```bash
helm install argo argo/argo-workflows --namespace argo --create-namespace --version v4.0.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Argo Workflows pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n argo
```

steps.title: Port-forward the Argo UI
steps.description:
To access the Argo Workflows UI, port-forward the service:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/argo-server -n argo 2746:2746
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for workflows
steps.description:
You can set resource limits for the workflows by modifying the `workflow-controller-configmap`:
```bash
kubectl edit configmap workflow-controller-configmap -n argo
# Add or modify the following lines:
# resourceLimits:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Argo Workflows will show the Argo server pod running in the `argo` namespace, and you will be able to access the Argo UI at `http://localhost:2746`. Additionally, you will have configured resource limits for your workflows.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add argo https://argoproj.github.io/argo-helm
helm repo update
helm install argo argo/argo-workflows --namespace argo --create-namespace --version v4.0.1

kubectl edit configmap workflow-controller-configmap -n argo
# Add or modify the following lines:
# resourceLimits:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Argo Workflows release from the `argo` namespace using Helm. This will remove all associated resources except for CRDs and persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall argo --namespace argo
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were installed with Argo Workflows. This step will also clean up any persistent volumes if they were created.
```bash
kubectl delete crd workflows.argoproj.io
kubectl delete crd workflowtemplates.argoproj.io
kubectl delete crd cronworkflows.argoproj.io
kubectl delete crd clusterworkflowtemplates.argoproj.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the `argo` namespace to clean up any remaining resources and verify that it has been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace argo
kubectl get namespaces | grep argo
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your workflows and configurations. You can export the current workflows and configurations using the following command:
```bash
kubectl get workflows -n argo -o yaml > argo-workflows-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Argo Workflows installation to the latest version. Replace `v4.0.2` with the desired version if necessary.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade argo argo/argo-workflows --namespace argo --version v4.0.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Argo Workflows pods are running with the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n argo
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see pods in a `CrashLoopBackOff` state, check the logs for the specific pod to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n argo
```
Common causes include misconfigured workflows or insufficient resources.

troubleshooting.title: Argo UI not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Argo UI, ensure that the port-forward command is running. Check the service status:
```bash
kubectl get svc argo-server -n argo
```
If the service is not running, you may need to troubleshoot the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Workflows not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If workflows are not starting, check the workflow controller logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs deployment/workflow-controller -n argo
```
Ensure that the controller is running and that there are no permission issues.

troubleshooting.title: Failed to create persistent volume claims
troubleshooting.description:
If your workflows require persistent volumes and they are failing to bind, check the PVC status:
```bash
kubectl get pvc -n argo
```
Ensure that there are available persistent volumes that match the requested storage class and size.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
